en.extract:
The copperback quail-thrush (Cinclosoma clarum) is a species of bird in the family Cinclosomatidae. It was split from the chestnut quail-thrush in 2015. It is endemic to Australia. Its natural habitat is Mediterranean-type shrubby vegetation.


Subspecies
C. c. clarum: little to no sexual dimorphism. They tend to be brown with rust-colored back bands.
C. c. fordianum: does exhibit sexual dimorphism. Males are gray with dark brown streaks; females are usually brownish-gray with similar patterning. Some females have a faint, narrow back band, and some have none at all.
C. c. morgani: Eyre Peninsula


References

The '''copperback quail-thrush''' ('''''Cinclosoma clarum''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Cinclosomatidae]]. It was split from the [[chestnut quail-thrush]] in 2015.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Dolman|first=Gaynor|author2=Joseph, Leo|date=2015|title=Evolutionary history of birds across southern Australia: structure, history and taxonomic implications of mitochondrial DNA diversity in an ecologically diverse suite of species|journal=Emu|volume=115|issue=1|pages=35–48|doi=10.1071/MU14047|bibcode=2015EmuAO.115...35D }}</ref> It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Australia]]. Its natural [[habitat]] is Mediterranean-type shrubby vegetation.

== Subspecies ==

* ''C. c. clarum'': little to no sexual dimorphism.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=McElroy |first1=Kerensa |last2=Black |first2=Andrew |last3=Dolman |first3=Gaynor |last4=Horton |first4=Philippa |last5=Pedler |first5=Lynn |last6=Campbell |first6=Catriona D. |last7=Drew |first7=Alex |last8=Joseph |first8=Leo |date=2020-05-31 |title=Robbery in progress: Historical museum collections bring to light a mitochondrial capture within a bird species widespread across southern Australia, the Copperback Quail-thrush ''Cinclosoma clarum'' |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/ece3.6403 |journal=Ecology and Evolution |volume=10 |issue=13 |pages=6785–6793 |doi=10.1002/ece3.6403 |pmid=32724551 |issn=2045-7758|pmc=7381587 |bibcode=2020EcoEv..10.6785M }}</ref> They tend to be brown with rust-colored back bands.<ref name=":0" />
* ''C. c. fordianum'': does exhibit sexual dimorphism.<ref name=":0" /> Males are gray with dark brown streaks; females are usually brownish-gray with similar patterning.<ref name=":0" /> Some females have a faint, narrow back band, and some have none at all.<ref name=":0" />
* ''C. c. morgani'': [[Eyre Peninsula]]
